quote: Each sandbox runs in isolation, giving it a dedicated kernel, filesystem, network stack, and allocated vCPU, RAM, and disk.
Sandboxes have 1 vCPU, 1GB RAM, and 3GiB disk by default.
A sandbox can have several different states. Each state reflects the current status of your sandbox.
Daytona provides comprehensive file system operations through the fs module in sandboxes.
Daytona provides process and code execution capabilities through the process module in sandboxes.
Log streaming allows you to access and process logs as they are being produced.
getSessionCommandLogs streams separate stdout and stderr callbacks for an async session command.
PTY sessions allow you to create interactive terminal sessions that can execute commands, handle user input, and manage terminal operations.
The SDK automatically looks for DAYTONA_API_KEY, DAYTONA_API_URL, and DAYTONA_TARGET.
Snapshots are sandbox templates created from Docker or OCI compatible images.
Daytona provides network egress limiting for sandboxes to control internet access.
Daytona API keys authenticate requests to the Daytona API.
Rate limits control how many API requests you can make within a specific time window.
Any process listening for HTTP traffic on ports 3000 - 9999 can be previewed through a generated URL.
When creating a sandbox, you can target a specific region.
Use the /home/daytona directory instead of /workspace for file operations.
For each user prompt it receives, it creates a new Daytona process session to run the agent command asynchronously and stream back the output.

locator: npm view @daytona/sdk version dist-tags exports dependencies
quote: npm view reported latest @daytona/sdk version 0.175.0 and ESM/CJS exports with bundled type declarations.
spawn is optional and only backends that can honestly expose unbuffered stdio implement it.
createBashTool composes backend, observeSandboxFileEvents, withBashExceptionCatch, and withAbortSignal.
executeCommand and writeFiles are bracketed by find -print0 plus sha256sum snapshots.
Docker spawn returns stdout, stderr, and exit from a child process backed by docker exec.
The Docker spawn test requires first stdout chunk before child exit, abort signal behavior, cwd, env, and file-event recording.
The prior sandbox design rejects buffered APIs as fake streaming and requires a real spawn primitive.
